text: Hello and welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're going to focus on ETEP retrorectus and how to manage posterior defects.

The case is a seventy-five-year-old man with a history of colonic perforation who had a lap converted to open colectomy. He presented with a medical history notable for COPD and diabetes, though these were optimized prior to the surgery.
Let's jump into the cross-sectional imaging. You'll note a very wide rectus on both sides, favorable anatomy for ETEP.

I think they measured around nine to ten centimeters, and then multifocal defects and diastasis in the midline. One of the other things I noted on this cross-sectional imaging was that the patient had a relative lack in preperitoneal fat, especially around the area of the hernias.
And so I definitely was not too enthusiastic about a transabdominal approach or a preperitoneal approach.

Hernia dimensions were six by eleven, thin peritoneum with multifocal defects. So in terms of the case setup, planned for a left retromuscular lateral doc with ETEP approach. And then we're sort of mentally prepared for a thin peritoneum.
The surgeon uses an optical port and an applied medical first entry trocar for initial access in the ETEP approach, allowing for insufflation while the inner cannula remains inside.
The surgeon performs initial insufflation with low flow in the retromuscular space, gradually establishing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon enters the areolar plane and places trocars sequentially on the lateral aspect of the rectus space. Laparoscopic instruments are then used to begin the initial dissection.

I like to use a lot of energy and minimize any bleeding, so I take my time here to just minimize the blood loss and make sure that my dissection plane is very clean and tidy, which helps me approach this robotically and not have to use any suction irrigation or anything of that nature.
The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon prepares to use standard tools, including a force bipolar and a monopolar scissor, for the procedure.
The surgeon examines the area around the suspected hernia, noting subtle differences in the fascia compared to the opposite side.
The surgeon incises the posterior sheath, leaving a one-centimeter cuff, and proceeds with pre-peritoneal dissection, focusing on areas where the tissue separates most easily.

The surgeon observes scarring and a non-functional pre-peritoneal fat pad, indicating potential proximity to the thin peritoneum of the hernia sac. The surgeon adjusts the approach to work more cephalad.
The surgeon works to develop the retromuscular space on the contralateral rectus, progressing higher to create space for eventual crossover.
The surgeon carefully teases the peritoneum away from the sheath to avoid entering the peritoneal cavity, revealing a substantial amount of preperitoneal fat in the upper region.
A peritoneum or a very wide diastasis that goes away from the preperitoneal fat, it can save you the pain of getting into the visceral cavity too early. And so here we are.
The surgeon continues the dissection and upon adjusting the camera view, a hole in the thin peritoneum becomes visible.
The surgeon carefully recruits the hernia sac, attempting to preserve as much of it as possible despite its thinness, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully works on recruiting and pulling down the hernia sac, avoiding buzzing across it to preserve tissue.

The surgical field shows a significant amount of tissue, which will aid in facilitating the eventual posterior closure. The surgeon prepares to close the peritoneum and the visceral sac as part of the ETAP procedure.
The surgeon unifies the inferior and superior dissection within the retro-rectus space, focusing on the contralateral rectus muscle without attempting sheath-to-sheath re-approximation.
The surgeon begins the contralateral retromuscular dissection, panning the camera to reveal multifocal defects, diastasis, a prominent hernia identified on CT scan, and an umbilical hernia.

The surgeon pans the camera to reveal the left-sided dissection, which appears solid, while the right side shows a significant amount of work remaining in the lower abdomen. A large posterior defect is visible, characterized by a prominent hole.

I think the first thing that you have to do is a short, adequate retrorectus dissection. We already saw what I like to do with recruitment of the sac. You can also use autologous tissue like omentum or falciform, though these are difficult to do in the robotic setting. In scenarios where the sac is still tight, I think releasing the posterior lamina of the internal abdominal oblique can be advantageous, and it doesn't really mean that you get a formal tar.

Bridging absorbable mesh can work, though this again is kind of cumbersome in the robotic setting, and then transversus abdominis release can be done, but I try to avoid releasing the transversus abdominis for a posterior issue. So here we go.
The surgeon begins mobilizing tissue, performing a wide dissection around the retrorectus space. The posterior area is visibly tented up, indicating tension in the tissue.

It's not where we want it to be. And in my head I'm wondering whether or not I'm going to have to do some advanced maneuvers. to close this posterior. One of the things that's not depicted in this video was that we did recruit that sac and you're not seeing it there, but it was hanging into the peritoneal cavity.

It will become more apparent later in this video. So really developing a full dissection well beyond the margins of the hernia making sure that we offload as much tension as possible from this posterior because we don't want tension.
The surgeon works on the inferior aspect of the contralateral rectus muscle, progressing towards the epigastric vessels.
The surgeon unifies the retromuscular space from the left and right sides, performing a satisfying dissection with minimal bleeding and surgical history visible.

The surgeon has mobilized the right rectus muscle, and the flap is now laying flat on the viscera. Further dissection is performed below the arcuate line, resulting in a loose flap that can be closed without tension.

Again, if there was any other tension, I would be Very hesitant to proceed with closure. I used to drop the insufflation pressure during this portion of the case. I actually now do not drop the insufflation pressure. I like to keep it between twelve and fifteen. It gives me a visual assessment of the tension to make sure that I don't have too much when closing.

The surgeon completes the closure of the posterior layer and begins the anterior fascial closure, starting below the hernia defect. The lower diastasis is placated, followed by sequential tightening of the fascia.

I'm using here a number one Stratafix. I recently started using it, and I must admit I really like this suture. It comes on a CT-one needle, and so it is a little challenging to pass through an eight millimeter port. As long as you inform your bedside assistant about it, I think it works fine. With regard to lengths that they have this manufactured, I have an eighteen inch, but there is twenty-four and twelve, as well as nine.

The surgeon performs sequential tightening of the anterior fascia using a suture in the robotic setting, observing the fascia coming together smoothly. The surgical field shows minimal sac tissue, requiring little placation.

The surgeon runs the sutures back on themselves to ensure complete re-approximation of the muscle, using three 18-inch Stratafix sutures. Smaller bites are taken, resulting in the muscle coming together smoothly.

The surgeon places a 22 by 26 centimeter Bartzoff's macroporous polypropylene mesh into the wide retro-rectus space. Basic tissue fixation is performed to the posterior, potentially offloading tension, while a sprayer is used for application.

This is really dependent on the bedside assistant and how familiar they are with the technique, but it went well. Uncomplicated case. This was a late case for me in the day, so the patient did stay overnight but was discharged on post-op day one. Thank you very much. Hopefully you enjoyed it.
